text: This article examines the contemporary linguistic landscape of Cieszyn and Český Těšín, sibling cities divided by the Olza River since 1920. Following a brief historical overview of Těšín Silesia’s multi-ethnic heritage and an outline of current Czech language policies regarding the Polish minority in the Zaolzie region, the author investigates how bilingualism manifests in public spaces today. The study analyzes authentic linguistic material to explore the nuances of both official and informal communication on both sides of the border. Specifically, the author evaluates the quality of written and spoken language through the lens of lexical borrowings, diminutive usage, interlingual homonymy (false friends), and graphical elements such as orthography and font styles. By situating these texts within their cultural and pragmatic contexts, the article identifies the intended recipients of Polish signage in Czechia and Czech advertising in Poland. Ultimately, the research reveals how the visual and communicative environment of these twin cities reflects broader differences in national minority rights, commercial interests, and the unique cross-border identity of the region’s inhabitants.
